title: Half the U.S. Trade Deficit Is China — and Economists Keep Missing Why It Matters

source.original: Tariffs, DOGE & Fixing America: Peter Thiel's Skeptic Solutions

body_html:
Peter Thiel frames the current trade confrontation with China not as conventional protectionism but as a geopolitical imperative that mainstream economists systematically underweight. The numbers tell a striking story: roughly a quarter of the U.S. trade deficit is bilateral with China, but another quarter is indirectly Chinese — components manufactured in China, shipped to third countries for assembly, then exported to America. China effectively accounts for half the problem.

The critical distinction Thiel draws is between economic rivals and geopolitical threats. When Germany sells Mercedes cars to the U.S., there's a legitimate argument about wanting those well-paying manufacturing jobs domestically. But Germany isn't going to repurpose its car factories to build tanks aimed at America. China presents a fundamentally different calculus: the dual-use problem, where economic capacity directly translates into military capability, makes every dollar of trade deficit a strategic vulnerability.

Thiel supports a radical trade reset where the U.S. not only restructures its own relationship with China but induces allied nations to do the same, potentially building a stronger Western alliance in the process. He acknowledges that some supply chain shifts — moving production from China to Vietnam, for example — represent meaningful wins even if they don't bring manufacturing home, because Vietnam, while communist, isn't attempting global hegemony.

On the question of whether AI and automation could enable reshoring, Thiel is characteristically precise. Manufacturing has been steadily automating for 250 years. AI may represent a quantum leap or merely a continuation of that trend. But reshoring also requires dismantling domestic anti-industrial policies — environmental regulations and other political blockers — that make American manufacturing uncompetitive independent of labor costs.
